# Introduction
Following [Chapter 15](https://inarwhal.github.io/NetworkAnalysisR-book/ch15-coevolution-networks-behaviors-siena-saom-R.html), we will here focus on problems related to peer influence and selection using the same data set as in the lecture, but instead focusing on drinking behavior (rather than smoking as in the lecture). 

Note that we are performing stochastic modeling so your output will deviate somewhat to mine or your neighbour's.

## Data: The Teenage Friends and Lifestyle Study
The data is ready for download int two file, one including the three waves of friendship as an array (binary), one containing the changing covariate alcohol drinking behavior (values 1–5), and once containing the constant covariate gender (1=Boys, 2=Girls).


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(RSiena)
library(statnet)
library(igraph)
library(ggraph)
library(intergraph)
library(patchwork)
library(networkdata)
library(graphlayouts)

# import networks for 3 waves:
networks <- readRDS("friendship1-3.rds")
#check dimensions
dim(networks)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 160 160   3
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# import alcohol attribute
alcohol <- readRDS("alcohol.rds")
head(alcohol)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     t1 t2 t3
s001  3  1  3
s002 NA NA NA
s003  2  2  2
s004  2  2  3
s005  2  3  3
s006  3  2  5
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# import gender attribute
gender <- readRDS("gender.rds")
head(gender)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
s001 s002 s003 s004 s005 s006 
   2    1    2    1    1    1 
```


:::
:::

There  are some missing data in a given wave, around 13% missing on key variables, like alcohol use, excluding students who were not in the school for that wave, having entered at a later wave or already exiting the school; this is about 4% of students. (It is generally considered problematic if one has more than 20% missing data. See RSiena manual for more details.)

For this analysis, we will keep all students who were ever in the school, thus allowing students to join and leave the network through time. This is handled by the structural zeros in the matrices, showing that no tie was possible from $i$ to $j$ and $j$ to $i$ because (at least) one of the students was not in the school for that wave. This is already part of the imported network data.

In RSiena, a value of 10 (in the input matrix) is a special value, indicating a structural zero is present. This is already properly coded in the friendship matrices. The model will thus be conditioned on the structural zeros present in the data. Similarly, we will not exclude cases with missing data, keeping the missing values as NAs (these values will be treated as non-informative during estimation).


## Creation of Siena Objects
We can now create a siena object based on the array of network data.

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
friendship <- sienaDependent(netarray = networks, type = "oneMode") 
friendship
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Type         oneMode              
Observations 3                    
Nodeset      Actors (160 elements)
```


:::
:::


Next we deal with the behavioral outcome of interest, alcohol use. Here we create the behavioral siena object.

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
alcohol_depvar <- sienaDependent(netarray = alcohol, type = "behavior") 
alcohol_depvar
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Type         behavior             
Observations 3                    
Nodeset      Actors (160 elements)
```


:::
:::


Let's also create the constant gender covariate:

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
gender <- coCovar(gender) 
```
:::


Finally, we put the constructed objects together using a `sienaDataCreate()` function:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
friend_behavior_covar <- sienaDataCreate(friendship, alcohol_depvar, gender)
friend_behavior_covar
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Dependent variables:  friendship, alcohol_depvar 
Number of observations: 3 

Nodeset                  Actors 
Number of nodes             160 

Dependent variable friendship      
Type               oneMode         
Observations       3               
Nodeset            Actors          
Densities          0.023 0.021 0.02

Dependent variable alcohol_depvar
Type               behavior      
Observations       3             
Nodeset            Actors        
Range              1 - 5         

Constant covariates:  gender 
```


:::
:::


## Model Specification and Estimation
We here specify our model of interest. We want to run a model where the focus is on selection/influence effects, here for drinking behavior. We start with the base model.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
siena_effects_3wave_example <- getEffects(friend_behavior_covar)
siena_effects_3wave_example 
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
  name           effectName                          include fix   test 
1 friendship     constant friendship rate (period 1) TRUE    FALSE FALSE
2 friendship     constant friendship rate (period 2) TRUE    FALSE FALSE
3 friendship     outdegree (density)                 TRUE    FALSE FALSE
4 friendship     reciprocity                         TRUE    FALSE FALSE
5 alcohol_depvar rate alcohol_depvar (period 1)      TRUE    FALSE FALSE
6 alcohol_depvar rate alcohol_depvar (period 2)      TRUE    FALSE FALSE
7 alcohol_depvar alcohol_depvar linear shape         TRUE    FALSE FALSE
8 alcohol_depvar alcohol_depvar quadratic shape      TRUE    FALSE FALSE
  initialValue parm
1    7.41692   0   
2    6.16651   0   
3   -1.66821   0   
4    0.00000   0   
5    0.76577   0   
6    1.15233   0   
7    0.35525   0   
8    0.00000   0   
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
effectsDocumentation(siena_effects_3wave_example)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Effects documentation written to file siena_effects_3wave_example.html .
```


:::
:::


Note that we now have rate parameters (controlling for change in ties over time) for T1 to T2 and T2 to T3. Now we add a term for peer influence using `avSim` (do people tend to move towards their friends drinking behavior?).

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
siena_effects_3wave_example <- includeEffects(siena_effects_3wave_example, 
                                              avSim, 
                                              interaction1 = "friendship", 
                                              name = "alcohol_depvar")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
  effectNumber effectName                        shortName include fix   test 
1 722          alcohol_depvar average similarity avSim     TRUE    FALSE FALSE
  initialValue parm
1          0   0   
```


:::
:::


Also, let's include a term that controls for the main effect of gender on drinking behavior.

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
siena_effects_3wave_example <- includeEffects(siena_effects_3wave_example, 
                                              effFrom, 
                                              interaction1 = "gender", 
                                              name = "alcohol_depvar")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
  effectNumber effectName                         shortName include fix   test 
1 835          alcohol_depvar: effect from gender effFrom   TRUE    FALSE FALSE
  initialValue parm
1          0   0   
```


:::
:::

Next,  we include terms that predict friendship ties as the outcome of interest, where students form and drop ties between T1 and T2 and T2 and T3. We will include outdegree and indegree effects for drinking (do people who drink send out/receive more ties?).

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
siena_effects_3wave_example <- includeEffects(siena_effects_3wave_example, 
                                              egoX, 
                                              interaction1 = "alcohol_depvar", 
                                              name = "friendship")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
  effectNumber effectName         shortName include fix   test  initialValue
1 463          alcohol_depvar ego egoX      TRUE    FALSE FALSE          0  
  parm
1 0   
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
siena_effects_3wave_example <- includeEffects(siena_effects_3wave_example, 
                                              altX, 
                                              interaction1 = "alcohol_depvar", 
                                              name = "friendship")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
  effectNumber effectName           shortName include fix   test  initialValue
1 448          alcohol_depvar alter altX      TRUE    FALSE FALSE          0  
  parm
1 0   
```


:::
:::

And now we add a term capturing the tendency for transitive closure in tie formation.

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
siena_effects_3wave_example <- includeEffects(siena_effects_3wave_example, 
                                              transTrip, name = "friendship")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
  effectNumber effectName          shortName include fix   test  initialValue
1 23           transitive triplets transTrip TRUE    FALSE FALSE          0  
  parm
1 0   
```


:::
:::


We take a look at the model effects:

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
siena_effects_3wave_example
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   name           effectName                          include fix   test 
1  friendship     constant friendship rate (period 1) TRUE    FALSE FALSE
2  friendship     constant friendship rate (period 2) TRUE    FALSE FALSE
3  friendship     outdegree (density)                 TRUE    FALSE FALSE
4  friendship     reciprocity                         TRUE    FALSE FALSE
5  friendship     transitive triplets                 TRUE    FALSE FALSE
6  friendship     alcohol_depvar alter                TRUE    FALSE FALSE
7  friendship     alcohol_depvar ego                  TRUE    FALSE FALSE
8  alcohol_depvar rate alcohol_depvar (period 1)      TRUE    FALSE FALSE
9  alcohol_depvar rate alcohol_depvar (period 2)      TRUE    FALSE FALSE
10 alcohol_depvar alcohol_depvar linear shape         TRUE    FALSE FALSE
11 alcohol_depvar alcohol_depvar quadratic shape      TRUE    FALSE FALSE
12 alcohol_depvar alcohol_depvar average similarity   TRUE    FALSE FALSE
13 alcohol_depvar alcohol_depvar: effect from gender  TRUE    FALSE FALSE
   initialValue parm
1     7.41692   0   
2     6.16651   0   
3    -1.66821   0   
4     0.00000   0   
5     0.00000   0   
6     0.00000   0   
7     0.00000   0   
8     0.76577   0   
9     1.15233   0   
10    0.35525   0   
11    0.00000   0   
12    0.00000   0   
13    0.00000   0   
```


:::
:::


Here we set up our control inputs. We set the max degree to 6 as students were limited to naming 6 friends.

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
input_options_3wave <- sienaAlgorithmCreate(MaxDegree = c(friendship = 6),
                                            n3 = 3000, seed = 5000) 
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
If you use this algorithm object, siena07 will create/use an output file Siena.txt .
```


:::
:::


And now we estimate the model.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
mod1_3wave_example <- siena07(input_options_3wave, 
                              data = friend_behavior_covar, 
                              effects = siena_effects_3wave_example, 
                              returnDeps = TRUE, initC = TRUE, 
                              useCluster = TRUE, nbrNodes = 2) 
mod1_3wave_example
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Estimates, standard errors and convergence t-ratios

                                               Estimate   Standard   Convergence 
                                                            Error      t-ratio   
Network Dynamics 
   1. rate constant friendship rate (period 1) 15.3341  ( 1.5272   )   -0.0280   
   2. rate constant friendship rate (period 2) 11.9472  ( 1.1155   )    0.0211   
   3. eval outdegree (density)                 -2.4778  ( 0.0445   )    0.0604   
   4. eval reciprocity                          2.0388  ( 0.0970   )    0.0303   
   5. eval transitive triplets                  0.5684  ( 0.0230   )    0.0108   
   6. eval alcohol_depvar alter                -0.0564  ( 0.0497   )   -0.0083   
   7. eval alcohol_depvar ego                   0.0266  ( 0.0609   )    0.0251   

Behavior Dynamics
   8. rate rate alcohol_depvar (period 1)       1.5476  ( 0.2826   )   -0.0282   
   9. rate rate alcohol_depvar (period 2)       2.5867  ( 0.5061   )   -0.0008   
  10. eval alcohol_depvar linear shape          0.4976  ( 0.1443   )   -0.0371   
  11. eval alcohol_depvar quadratic shape       0.1025  ( 0.1079   )   -0.0293   
  12. eval alcohol_depvar average similarity    7.7107  ( 2.2675   )    0.0152   
  13. eval alcohol_depvar: effect from gender   0.0511  ( 0.2140   )   -0.0026   

Overall maximum convergence ratio:    0.1026 


Degrees constrained to maximum values:
friendship : 6 


Total of 4494 iteration steps.
```


:::
:::


The first thing we see is that the model appears to have converged (looking at the overall convergence ratio and the t-ratios for each term).   The estimate on average similarity (7.7707) is high and the standard error is over 3 times smaller. The effect for peer influence is clearly significant here (with a t-statistic of: 7.7707 / 2.5383 = 3.0614). We also see that reciprocity is quite strong, as is the tendency for transitive relations. We also see that there is selection on alcohol use, suggesting that not only do students tend to converge to the behaviors of their peers, they also tend to seek out those with similar drinking behavior.

## Goodness of Fit
It is a good idea (like with ERGM) to check the fit of the model. Here, we ask if the estimated model can reproduce key features of the network and behaviors. The basic idea is to simulate from the model and then check if the networks and behavioral distributions based on the model match the observed data.

Let's first see if the model is reproducing the indegree distribution. The function is `sienaGOF()`. The arguments are:

- `sienaFitObject` = model,
- `auxiliaryFunction` = function to calculate
- `varName` = network relation or behavior of interest




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
gof1 <- sienaGOF(mod1_3wave_example, IndegreeDistribution, 
                 verbose = TRUE, join = TRUE, varName = "friendship") 
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Calculating auxiliary statistics for periods  1 2 .
```


:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(gof1) 
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](saoms-2_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-14-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::

Here we look at the overall fit of the behavior distribution.

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
gof_behaviour <- sienaGOF(mod1_3wave_example, BehaviorDistribution,
                          varName = "alcohol_depvar")
plot(gof_behaviour)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Note: some statistics are not plotted because their variance is 0.
This holds for the statistic: 5.
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](saoms-2_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-15-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::

What do you conclude in terms of goodness of fit here?

## Time Heterogeneity
When ther are more than two time periods, it is important to test for time heterogeneity, asking if the estimates are different from T1 to T2 compared to T2 and T3 (in the case of three waves). Let's test whether the estimates differ from T1 to T2 vs. T2 to T3 using the `sienaTimeTest()` function. The input is the estimated model.

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
timetest <- sienaTimeTest(mod1_3wave_example)
summary(timetest) 
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Joint significance test of time heterogeneity:
chi-squared =  11.55, d.f. = 9, p= 0.2398, 
where H0: The following parameters are zero:
 (1) (*)Dummy2:outdegree (density)
 (2) (*)Dummy2:reciprocity
 (3) (*)Dummy2:transitive triplets
 (4) (*)Dummy2:alcohol_depvar alter
 (5) (*)Dummy2:alcohol_depvar ego
 (6) (*)Dummy2:alcohol_depvar linear shape
 (7) (*)Dummy2:alcohol_depvar quadratic shape
 (8) (*)Dummy2:alcohol_depvar average similarity
 (9) (*)Dummy2:alcohol_depvar: effect from gender

Individual significance tests and one-step estimators:
                                             Initial Est. One Step Est. p-Value
outdegree (density)                          -2.4778      -2.4617        0.0000
reciprocity                                   2.0388       1.9375        0.0000
transitive triplets                           0.5684       0.5265        0.0000
alcohol_depvar alter                         -0.0564      -0.0253        0.2560
alcohol_depvar ego                            0.0266      -0.0639        0.6620
alcohol_depvar linear shape                   0.4976       0.5438        0.0010
alcohol_depvar quadratic shape                0.1025       0.2367        0.3420
alcohol_depvar average similarity             7.7107       9.4871        0.0010
alcohol_depvar: effect from gender            0.0511      -0.0338        0.8110
(*)Dummy2:outdegree (density)                 0.0000      -0.0758        0.0730
(*)Dummy2:reciprocity                         0.0000       0.2122        0.0130
(*)Dummy2:transitive triplets                 0.0000       0.1033        0.0040
(*)Dummy2:alcohol_depvar alter                0.0000      -0.0430        0.7390
(*)Dummy2:alcohol_depvar ego                  0.0000       0.1527        0.3430
(*)Dummy2:alcohol_depvar linear shape         0.0000      -0.0505        0.9610
(*)Dummy2:alcohol_depvar quadratic shape      0.0000      -0.2402        0.4130
(*)Dummy2:alcohol_depvar average similarity   0.0000      -3.1384        0.9020
(*)Dummy2:alcohol_depvar: effect from gender  0.0000       0.0876        0.9820

Effect-wise joint significance tests
(i.e. each effect across all dummies):
                                   chi-sq. df p-value
outdegree (density)                   3.20  1   0.074
reciprocity                           6.19  1   0.013
transitive triplets                   8.12  1   0.004
alcohol_depvar alter                  0.11  1   0.740
alcohol_depvar ego                    0.90  1   0.343
alcohol_depvar linear shape           0.00  1   1.000
alcohol_depvar quadratic shape        0.67  1   0.413
alcohol_depvar average similarity     0.02  1   0.888
alcohol_depvar: effect from gender    0.00  1   1.000

Period-wise joint significance tests
(i.e. each period across all parameters):
         chi-sq. df p-value
Period 1   11.55  9    0.24
Period 2   11.55  9    0.24

Use the following indices for plotting:
  (1) outdegree (density)
  (2) reciprocity
  (3) transitive triplets
  (4) alcohol_depvar alter
  (5) alcohol_depvar ego
  (6) alcohol_depvar linear shape
  (7) alcohol_depvar quadratic shape
  (8) alcohol_depvar average similarity
  (9) alcohol_depvar: effect from gender

If you would like to fit time dummies to your model,
use the includeTimeDummy function.
Type "?test_time" for more information on this output.
```


:::
:::

The main test we are interested in is the 'joint significance' test. The null hypothesis is that the effects are the same across the waves. A $p$-value < .05 would offer evidence to reject that null (meaning the effects may differ across waves). Here there is not strong evidence of time differences.
